policy_statement: Reduce emissions from deforestation and peatland (peat decomposition and peat fire).
mechanism_description: The policy pursues reductions in deforestation and peat decomposition and fires to avoid releasing stored carbon and to help keep land ecosystems as a net carbon sink.
primary_causal_pathway: Ecosystem protection/restoration → ↓ biomass & soil/peat carbon loss + ↓ fire/oxidation emissions → ↓ net GHG emissions (and preserved carbon sinks)
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Changed activity/system: Land management and land-use decisions are shifted to avoid forest clearing, drainage, and conversion of peatlands, and to prevent/limit peat and forest fires.  
(2) Mediating process: Reduced oxidation of drained peat, reduced combustion emissions from fires, and reduced loss of biomass/soil carbon; continued photosynthetic uptake and storage maintain or enhance ecosystem carbon stocks.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Avoided CO₂ emissions (dominant), plus reduced non-CO₂ emissions from fires and degradation; sustained/maintained net carbon sink function over time.
dominant_pathway_test: Preventing deforestation and peatland degradation avoids the release of large stored carbon stocks, yielding substantial net reductions in greenhouse-gas emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Ecosystem protection prevents carbon-stock loss and associated emissions, and preserves carbon sequestration capacity—i.e., a biophysical ecosystem-carbon pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the outcome is clearly mitigation-relevant, the mechanism works through conserving/restoring natural ecosystems (forests/peatlands) rather than mainly through energy/technology efficiency or engineered emissions controls, aligning most directly with Nature-Based Solutions.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some forest/peat policies also deliver adaptation (flood buffering, water regulation, heat reduction) and resource benefits (timber substitution, livelihoods), but the single biggest climate effect for this mechanism is typically avoided CO₂ from land-use change and peat oxidation/fire.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that reduces deforestation and peatland degradation/fire inherently preserves terrestrial carbon stocks and avoids land-sector emissions, so the Nature-Based Solutions primary category holds across implementations.  
5) Secondary subtype deferral: Any finer classification (e.g., protection vs restoration, REDD+ finance vs regulatory bans, adaptation co-benefits) requires policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level coding.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If an implementation is primarily an enforcement/accounting program without clear on-the-ground ecosystem protection outcomes, or if it is framed as industrial emissions control (rare for this mechanism), categorization could be revisited; otherwise this is a straightforward Nature-Based Solutions mechanism centered on avoided land-use emissions.
key_indicators: “significantly reduce the emission”; “turn it into net sink by 2050”; “by 2030”; “reducing emission from deforestation and peatland (peat decomposition and peat fire)”
co_benefits: Biodiversity protection; reduced haze/air pollution and public health impacts from fires; improved water regulation and reduced land subsidence; livelihood co-benefits for forest-dependent communities.
instance_edge_case_notes: No specific instrument is named (e.g., moratorium, protected areas, incentives), so classification is based on the explicit time-bound net-sink/emissions-reduction objective; if the broader CPOS/LCCP document contains mandated measures, instrument_type could shift to regulatory-mandate/incentive-program/planning-strategy accordingly.
